issues.description: Saying 'the story starts in 1953 with Watson, Crick, and a critical X-ray image taken by Rosalind Franklin' makes Franklin sound like she just snapped a photo. As an adversarial student I'd walk away thinking Watson and Crick did the science and Franklin was the lab technician. The script never says Franklin built the diffraction technique or that her measurements were used without her consent. Also: Maurice Wilkins (who shared the Nobel) is never mentioned, and the actual Erwin Chargaff who shows up two sections later is treated as a disembodied observation rather than a person. Inconsistent attribution.

The 'shape rule' is presented as if A and G being purines and T and C being pyrimidines is an additional rule on top of A-T / G-C — but actually it's the same constraint (two H-bonds work geometrically because purine + pyrimidine = consistent helix diameter). A student might come away thinking there are TWO independent rules to memorize. Could explicitly say 'this is why' the pairing works, not 'there is also.'

The narration says '5' and 3' come from carbons on the sugar' but never shows or names which carbons or where the phosphodiester bond forms. A student watching this without the slides has no mental model of WHY polymerase can only extend a 3' OH — it's just asserted. The connection 'because the phosphodiester bond forms between the 3' OH and the incoming 5' phosphate' is the explanation, and it's missing.

'The pattern matched only one model' — but the narration never says WHAT pattern! As a student I cannot answer the AP FRQ 'describe the results that ruled out the conservative model' from this script. Need at minimum: 'after one round, all DNA was intermediate density — that ruled out conservative; after two rounds, half was intermediate and half light — that ruled out dispersive.'

Topoisomerase is described as working 'ahead of the fork' but the mechanism ('cuts and rejoins to relieve supercoiling') is vague. A student might confuse this with helicase (which also acts at/near the fork). Worth one sentence on why unwinding causes supercoiling — twisting one part of a rope tightens the rest. Also: origin of replication is named only once and never explained; bacteria have one origin, eukaryotes have many. AP loves that distinction.

'Polymerase one later removes those primers' — but the narration in section 09 just established DNA polymerase III as the one adding nucleotides. A student hearing 'polymerase one' for the first time at this moment might think it's a typo for 'polymerase III' or the same enzyme. Pol I needs to be introduced before it's used, or the script should say 'a different enzyme, DNA polymerase I.'

Two issues. (1) The script attributes the entire 10^-9 error rate to polymerase proofreading, but mismatch repair (a separate downstream system) is responsible for most of the remaining accuracy. A student who memorizes 'proofreading gets it to 1 in a billion' will get burned on an FRQ that asks 'name two mechanisms that contribute to replication fidelity.' (2) The telomere/cancer link is interesting but skips the 'end replication problem' — the actual physical reason this happens. Why can't the lagging strand 'fully complete its end with a primer'? Because the final RNA primer at the very end has nothing 5' of it to extend from when removed. A motivated student will want that mechanism, not just the assertion.

'Most cancers reactivate it, which is part of what makes them immortal' — the word 'immortal' is vivid but a student might over-extrapolate: not every cancer uses telomerase (~10–15% use ALT, alternative lengthening of telomeres). Out of scope for AP, but the absolute phrasing is misleading. 'Many' or 'the majority' is safer than 'most.'
OpenStax Biology Chapter 14 is on DNA structure and replication — this is correct, BUT the narration assumes the student has access. No fallback for students without the textbook. Minor.

summary: The script is engaging and the AP-trap callouts at the end are gold, but it commits the classic explainer sin of asserting conclusions without showing the reasoning — most damagingly in the Meselson-Stahl section where the actual band pattern is never described. Pol I appears in the 'answer' before being introduced. Franklin gets credited as a photographer.
